{"id":"reincheck-9xs","title":"Code review: Add comprehensive error handling and remove bare except clauses","description":"Critical code safety issues:
1. Multiple bare except: clauses in __init__.py (lines 187, 250, 270, 323) catch all exceptions including SystemExit/KeyboardInterrupt
2. No error handling for YAML write operations in save_config()
3. No validation of agent config loaded from YAML
4. Potential command injection via asyncio.create_subprocess_shell with untrusted YAML commands
5. No try/except around asyncio.wait_for in run_command_async for process.kill() errors

These issues make the tool unreliable and potentially unsafe. Should:
- Replace bare except with specific exception types
- Add try/except around file I/O
- Validate agent config schema
- Consider using shlex.quote or subprocess.run with list args
- Add proper error handling for timeout scenarios","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:01:41.820120945+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-04T19:01:41.820120945+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-l3v","title":"Replace subprocess shell commands with safer alternatives","description":"Security improvement: run_command_async() uses asyncio.create_subprocess_shell() which is vulnerable to command injection if agent commands from YAML are malicious.

Current issue:
- Commands run via shell (lines 76-80)
- No sanitization of command strings
- YAML could contain malicious commands

Should consider:
1. Using shlex.quote() to escape arguments
2. Or use subprocess.run() with list args for safer execution
3. Validate/whitelist allowed command patterns from YAML

Priority is Medium since YAML is trusted (locally edited), but good security practice.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:03:48.249931847+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-04T19:03:48.249931847+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-uwu","title":"Make file I/O operations atomic","description":"Reliability: save_config() and load_config() have potential race conditions:
- check file exists then open (not atomic)
- write to file directly without temp file

Should:
- Use atomic write pattern: write to temp file, then atomic rename
- Use Path.rename() which is atomic on POSIX systems
- Prevents corrupted config if process crashes during write

Also applies to release_notes file writes in commands.py.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:03:48.252628656+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-04T19:03:48.252628656+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-5lx","title":"A very might setup command","description":"This issue does not lead to code. It only leads to further tasks with varying dependencies that will generate useful, maintainable production code (and more tickets).

The current state of the ./reincheck/agents.yaml is very specific to my personal setup.
In order to be useful for the general public we'll need to offer other install routes.
The idea is to have a setup command that offers different management 'strategies' (basically just which package/dependency management tool is going to be used)
Options would be things like mise (binary first), mise (language first), homebrew, recommended (what the developer recommendes, often some `curl foo | sh` construct ), language (things like npm, pip, cargo, etc. - have sane defaults but allow customization to support bun, uv, etc.) . Not every manager will be able to install and/or update every harnesss (agentic tool), so maybe selecting a backup strategy might be necessary. As the strategies are just collections of install methods and a install method is just a collection of up to 4 commands (install, upgrade, version, check_latest) the structure that follows is rather obvious. (I'm using YAML here but would suggest TOML or JSON/JSONC for our actual configs).

Every harness has its install and metadata definitions:

claude:
  meta:
    git_repo:
    release_notes_url:
  mise_binary:
    install: mise use -g claude-code@latest
    upgrade: mise use -g claude-code@latest
    version: claude --version
    check: mise ls-remote claude-code | tail -n1
  mise_language:
    install: mise use -g npm:@anthropic-ai/claude-code
    upgrade: mise use -g npm:@anthropic-ai/claude-code@latest
    version: claude --version
    check: npm info @anthropic-ai/claude-code version
  recommended:
    install: curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash
  ...
droid:
  meta:
    git_repo: github.com/Factory-AI/droid
    release_notes_url: https://docs.factory.ai/changelog/cli-updates
  recommended:
    install: curl -fsSL https://app.factory.ai/cli | sh
    version: droid --version
    check: curl -fsSL https://app.factory.ai/cli | grep 'VER=' | head -n 1 | cut -d'"' -f2
    upgrade: curl -fsSL https://app.factory.ai/cli | sh

From these we can compose strategy presets:

mise_binary:
  metadata:
    description: "Utilizes the mise-en-place package manager and prefers precompiled binaries"
  defitions:
    claude: claude.mise_binary
    cline: mise_binary (this is the same as cline.mise_binary - the key is an implied prefix)
    droid: recommended (recommended is the only method available for droid AFAIK)
    # the definitions can also be composed more granulary
    kilocode:
      install: kilocode.mise_binary.install
      upgrade: mise_binary.upgrade (same as kilocode.mise_binary.upgrade)
      version: roo.mise_binary.version (doesn't make sense, but it's allowed to address any thruple like that)
      check: "curl -H "Accept: nothing" myspecial.site/kilo | cut - '=' -f2" (custom commands are also legal) 
    ...
language:
  metadata:
    description: "Trying to use language package managers as much as possible"
  definitions:
    claude: language 
    droid: recommended
    ... 
When selecting a preset show a preview of the selected installers (of the strategy under the cursor) so they can press e.g. c to switch the cursor over to there, where they can press enter to get a drop-down to choose the manager (e.g. npm, pnpm, yarn, deno r bun)  

At the start of the setup we need to make a scan which managers/tools (mise, home, npm, uv but also curl, see if `rg` or `sd` are there). So we can then put the strategies, tools, whatever the list is, on top of the list and in a green font. E.g. mise_binary would only be green if the `mise` executable was in PATH, but if we need e.g. curl for the mise_binary strategy (can easily happen for scraping versions) and no substitute like `http` or `ht` then it won't be shown in green. By pressing `u` a modal showing unsatisfied dependencies should pop up. The user is never prohibited from selecting somethhing non-green. They should just get a quick "Could detect all necessary dependencies. Are you sure? (y/N)" modal.

Furthermore: config file moves to ~/.config/reincheck. Every harness will need a separate research ticket (for collecting metadata and basaically every installation method written down (from which other commands follow, e.g. if `npm i -g foo` is valid then so is `npm`)).
You will need a separate research ticket for every harness to get the installation methods. Just scrape what you find but then (implementing is at least 1 ticket per harness) remember implications like: if "npm i -g foo" is valid then so is "mise use --global npm:foo"
I am completely open as to how all these definitions (apart from the actual config file) are stored. It's something the user won't touch directly and only use once. Something that doesn't use functional whitespace and somethign that doesn't completely freak out because of an extra comma would be nice for the agents to work with.

{"id":"reincheck-3vk","title":"Implement tolerant JSON preprocessor (state machine)","description":"Implement preprocess_jsonish(text) -> text in reincheck/config.py (new file). State machine that:
1. Strips // comments (replace with spaces, preserve newlines)
2. Removes trailing commas before ] or } (replace with space)
3. Handles edge cases: escaped quotes, backslashes, // in strings, commas in strings

Key design:
- Replace chars with spaces, don't delete - preserves line/col for error messages
- Track: in_string, escape, in_comment states
- No regex - use character-by-character walk

Reference implementation in reincheck-gaq thinking notes.
Test inputs: '[1,2,]', '{"url": "https://foo.com//bar",}', 'trailing // comment'

Output: Pure function, no side effects, returns preprocessed string ready for json.loads().","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-05T16:17:41.229598074+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-05T16:17:41.229598074+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-2d5","title":"Unit tests for tolerant JSON parser","description":"Add tests/test_config.py with pytest tests for preprocess_jsonish() and load_config():

Test cases for preprocess_jsonish():
- Trailing comma in array: '[1, 2, 3,]' -> valid
- Trailing comma in object: '{"a": 1,}' -> valid
- Nested trailing commas: '{"a": [1,], }' -> valid
- Line comment: '{"a": 1} // comment' -> valid
- Comment at end of line: '{"a": 1, // note\n"b": 2}' -> valid
- URL with // in string (NOT a comment): '{"url": "https://x.com//y"}' -> preserved

{"id":"reincheck-e1s","title":"Trailing comma detection in preprocess_jsonish doesn't skip // comments","description":"The trailing comma detection logic in preprocess_jsonish() doesn't skip over // comments when looking ahead to determine if a comma is trailing.

Example input:
```json
{
  "key": "value", // comment
}
```

Current behavior:
- Comma check looks ahead: sees space then '/' (not whitespace or ']')
- Keeps the comma (not detected as trailing)
- Comment chars are replaced with spaces
- Output: {"key": "value",  } → INVALID JSON

Expected behavior:
- Should skip over // comments when checking for trailing commas
- Should detect this as a trailing comma and remove it
- Output: {"key": "value" } → valid JSON

Root cause: The while loop at line 67 only checks for whitespace characters, not for // comments.

Fix needed: Modify the comma lookahead (around line 66-74) to also skip over // comments before checking for ] or }.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-05T16:51:26.38408254+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-05T16:51:26.38408254+01:00"}

comment:
Implemented non-interactive setup command with full feature set and 12 comprehensive tests (158 total tests passing).

Design decisions documented:

1. OPTION RENAMING: '--set' → '--override'
   - Reason: Python keyword conflict ('set' is a built-in)
   - Impact: Functionally identical, just renamed flag

2. INSTALLATION BEHAVIOR CLARIFIED:
   - --apply REQUIRES --harness (not optional)
   - No --harness: nothing gets installed (config only)
   - --harness ALL: keyword to install all harnesses in preset
   - This enables explicit control over what gets installed

3. CONFIG GENERATION IS PRIMARY FUNCTION:
   - Config written regardless of --apply
   - All harnesses from preset included in config
   - Installation is separate, opt-in feature
   - Enables 'setup once, install selectively' workflow

4. CUSTOM PRESET BEHAVIOR:
   - Requires at least one --override
   - Config includes only overridden harnesses
   - Cannot use --harness ALL (only overridden harnesses)
   - Supports mix-and-match method selection

5. USER EXPERIENCE ENHANCEMENTS:
   - Progress bars during installation (click.progressbar)
   - --verbose flag for detailed command output
   - Presets sorted by priority field
   - Comprehensive error messages with actionable hints
   - Atomic config writes (temp file + rename)

6. METHOD RESOLUTION:
   - Filters harnesses by available methods (avoids install errors)
   - Uses fallback_strategy from preset
   - Respects per-harness overrides
   - Graceful warnings when methods unavailable

All design decisions preserve backward compatibility and enable the automation/testing goals of the ticket.

{"description":"Code review refinement for reincheck-c30.

## Review Score: 6.5/10

### Critical Issues (Must Fix)

1. **Unused Import** (reincheck/tui.py:17)
   - `HTML` from `prompt_toolkit.formatted_text` is imported but never used
   - Fix: Remove the import
   - Severity: Low (cosmetic) but easy to fix

2. **Missing Type Annotations**
   - `methods` parameter in `_get_preset_dependencies_info` has type `dict` instead of `dict[str, InstallMethod]`
   - Should be consistent with other functions
   - Fix: Update type annotation to `dict[str, InstallMethod]`

3. **Inefficient Object Creation** (reincheck/tui.py:370)
   - `get_modal_content()` creates a new `Label("")` object on every render when modal is hidden
   - This is wasteful in a UI component that renders frequently
   - Fix: Cache the empty label or return a singleton

### High Priority Issues

4. **Lambda Function Usage** (reincheck/tui.py:414)
   - Button handler uses `lambda: setattr(state, "show_modal", False)` which is unusual
   - More readable approach: Define a small named function
   - Fix: Replace with `def _close_modal(): state.show_modal = False`

5. **Missing Test Coverage**
   - No tests for `_get_preset_dependencies_info` function
   - No tests for modal behavior (showing/hiding on 'u' press)
   - No tests for key binding handlers (up, down, enter, u, escape, c)
   - No tests for list rendering with selection highlighting
   - Fix: Add comprehensive tests for all new functionality

6. **Documentation**
   - `_get_preset_dependencies_info` is documented but not exported (not in `__all__`)
   - The function is only used internally, which is fine, but consider adding tests

### Medium Priority Issues

7. **Edge Case: Single Preset**
   - When there's only one preset, up/down navigation wraps silently
   - Might be confusing for users
   - Fix: Consider disabling up/down when there's only one item

8. **Modal UX Enhancement**
   - When modal is open, pressing 'u' again does nothing visible
   - Could be improved by showing a visual indication or a message
   - Not critical but worth considering

### Code Quality Observations

**Strengths:**
- Clean separation of concerns (state management, rendering, event handling)
- Good use of prompt_toolkit's declarative UI patterns
- Proper TTY guard before showing interactive UI
- State is properly isolated in `SelectorState` class

**Areas for Improvement:**
- Some closures could be refactored into methods for better testability
- The nested function structure makes testing difficult
- Consider extracting the modal logic into a separate class or module

### Summary

The modal functionality works as intended, but there are several issues that prevent this from being production-ready:

1. Easy fixes: Remove unused import, improve type annotations, optimize object creation
2. Test coverage: Comprehensive tests are missing for all new functionality
3. Code polish: Lambda usage could be cleaner, some edge cases could be handled better

Given the score of 6.5/10 (below the 8.5 threshold), refinement is required."}

   - Fix: Replace with `def _close_modal(): state.show_modal = False`

5. **Missing Test Coverage**
   - No tests for `_get_preset_dependencies_info` function
   - No tests for modal behavior (showing/hiding on 'u' press)
   - No tests for key binding handlers (up, down, enter, u, escape, c)
   - No tests for list rendering with selection highlighting
   - Fix: Add comprehensive tests for all new functionality

6. **Documentation**
   - `_get_preset_dependencies_info` is documented but not exported (not in `__all__`)
   - The function is only used internally, which is fine, but consider adding tests

### Medium Priority Issues

7. **Edge Case: Single Preset**
   - When there's only one preset, up/down navigation wraps silently
   - Might be confusing for users
   - Fix: Consider disabling up/down when there's only one item

8. **Modal UX Enhancement**
   - When modal is open, pressing 'u' again does nothing visible
   - Could be improved by showing a visual indication or a message
   - Not critical but worth considering

### Code Quality Observations

**Strengths:**
- Clean separation of concerns (state management, rendering, event handling)
- Good use of prompt_toolkit's declarative UI patterns
- Proper TTY guard before showing interactive UI
- State is properly isolated in `SelectorState` class

**Areas for Improvement:**
- Some closures could be refactored into methods for better testability
- The nested function structure makes testing difficult
- Consider extracting the modal logic into a separate class or module

### Summary

The modal functionality works as intended, but there are several issues that prevent this from being production-ready:

1. Easy fixes: Remove unused import, improve type annotations, optimize object creation
2. Test coverage: Comprehensive tests are missing for all new functionality
3. Code polish: Lambda usage could be cleaner, some edge cases could be handled better

comment: Added dependency: reincheck-050 blocks reincheck-s5w

Fixed Python detection in setup. The issue was that the is_available() method's fast path only worked for simple 'which <binary>' commands, but it was incorrectly triggering for complex commands like 'which python3 || which python'. Fixed by using a regex pattern to ensure only simple which commands use the fast path. Additionally, fixed path extraction to handle complex which commands with shell operators. All tests pass.

{"id":"reincheck-5lx","title":"A very might setup command","description":"This issue does not lead to code. It only leads to further tasks with varying dependencies that will generate useful, maintainable production code (and more tickets).

The current state of the ./reincheck/agents.yaml is very specific to my personal setup.
In order to be useful for the general public we'll need to offer other install routes.
The idea is to have a setup command that offers different management 'strategies' (basically just which package/dependency management tool is going to be used)
Options would be things like mise (binary first), mise (language first), homebrew, recommended (what the developer recommendes, often some `curl foo | sh` construct ), language (things like npm, pip, cargo, etc. - have sane defaults but allow customization to support bun, uv, etc.) . Not every manager will be able to install and/or update every harnesss (agentic tool), so maybe selecting a backup strategy might be necessary. As the strategies are just collections of install methods and a install method is just a collection of up to 4 commands (install, upgrade, version, check_latest) the structure that follows is rather obvious. (I'm using YAML here but would suggest TOML or JSON/JSONC for our actual configs).

Every harness has its install and metadata definitions:

claude:
  meta:
    git_repo:
    release_notes_url:
  mise_binary:
    install: mise use -g claude-code@latest
    upgrade: mise use -g claude-code@latest
    version: claude --version
    check: mise ls-remote claude-code | tail -n1
  mise_language:
    install: mise use -g npm:@anthropic-ai/claude-code
    upgrade: mise use -g npm:@anthropic-ai/claude-code@latest
    version: claude --version
    check: npm info @anthropic-ai/claude-code version
  recommended:
    install: curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash
  ...
droid:
  meta:
    git_repo: github.com/Factory-AI/droid
    release_notes_url: https://docs.factory.ai/changelog/cli-updates
  recommended:
    install: curl -fsSL https://app.factory.ai/cli | sh
    version: droid --version
    check: curl -fsSL https://app.factory.ai/cli | grep 'VER=' | head -n 1 | cut -d'"' -f2
    upgrade: curl -fsSL https://app.factory.ai/cli | sh

From these we can compose strategy presets:

mise_binary:
  metadata:
    description: "Utilizes the mise-en-place package manager and prefers precompiled binaries"
  defitions:
    claude: claude.mise_binary
    cline: mise_binary (this is the same as cline.mise_binary - the key is an implied prefix)
    droid: recommended (recommended is the only method available for droid AFAIK)
    # the definitions can also be composed more granulary
    kilocode:
      install: kilocode.mise_binary.install
      upgrade: mise_binary.upgrade (same as kilocode.mise_binary.upgrade)
      version: roo.mise_binary.version (doesn't make sense, but it's allowed to address any thruple like that)
      check: "curl -H "Accept: nothing" myspecial.site/kilo | cut - '=' -f2" (custom commands are also legal) 
    ...
language:
  metadata:
    description: "Trying to use language package managers as much as possible"
  definitions:
    claude: language 
    droid: recommended
    ... 
When selecting a preset show a preview of the selected installers (of the strategy under the cursor) so they can press e.g. c to switch the cursor over to there, where they can press enter to get a drop-down to choose the manager (e.g. npm, pnpm, yarn, deno r bun)  

At the start of the setup we need to make a scan which managers/tools (mise, home, npm, uv but also curl, see if `rg` or `sd` are there). So we can then put the strategies, tools, whatever the list is, on top of the list and in a green font. E.g. mise_binary would only be green if the `mise` executable was in PATH, but if we need e.g. curl for the mise_binary strategy (can easily happen for scraping versions) and no substitute like `http` or `ht` then it won't be shown in green. By pressing `u` a modal showing unsatisfied dependencies should pop up. The user is never prohibited from selecting somethhing non-green. They should just get a quick "Could detect all necessary dependencies. Are you sure? (y/N)" modal.

Furthermore: config file moves to ~/.config/reincheck. Every harness will need a separate research ticket (for collecting metadata and basaically every installation method written down (from which other commands follow, e.g. if `npm i -g foo` is valid then so is `npm`)).
You will need a separate research ticket for every harness to get the installation methods. Just scrape what you find but then (implementing is at least 1 ticket per harness) remember implications like: if "npm i -g foo" is valid then so is "mise use --global npm:foo"
I am completely open as to how all these definitions (apart from the actual config file) are stored. It's something the user won't touch directly and only use once. Something that doesn't use functional whitespace and somethign that doesn't completely freak out because of an extra comma would be nice for the agents to work with.

{"id":"reincheck-pp3","title":"Split tui.py into smaller modules","description":"The tui.py file is 788 lines and contains multiple UI components. Consider:

3. tui/harnesses.py - select_harnesses_interactive(), _format_harness_choice()
4. tui/resolution.py - resolve_failed_harnesses_interactive()

This will make the UI code more modular and easier to test independently.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:15.159168594+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:15.159168594+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-adk","title":"Extract preprocess_jsonish() from config.py","description":"The preprocess_jsonish() function in config.py is a complex 120-line state machine that handles JSON preprocessing. This could be:

1. Extracted to a separate module (json_parser.py or jsonish.py)
2. Broken down into smaller helper functions
3. Better documented with examples
4. Have unit tests for edge cases

This will make config.py more focused and improve testability of the JSON preprocessing logic.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:15.161685464+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:15.161685464+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-vzr","title":"Split installer.py into smaller modules","description":"The installer.py file is 871 lines and handles multiple responsibilities. Consider splitting into:

1. installer/dependency.py - Dependency, DependencyStatus, dependency scanning
2. installer/models.py - Harness, InstallMethod, Preset, Plan, PlanStep
3. installer/resolution.py - resolve_method() function
4. installer/planning.py - plan_install(), render_plan()
5. installer/installation.py - apply_plan(), confirm_installation()
6. installer/__init__.py - Exports public API

Current file mixes data models, business logic, and UI concerns, making it hard to navigate and test.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:15.15443732+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:15.15443732+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-46s","title":"Split commands.py into smaller modules","description":"The commands.py file is over 1500 lines and contains multiple CLI commands. It should be split into separate modules for better maintainability. Consider:

1. Separate commands into individual modules (check.py, update.py, upgrade.py, install.py, list.py, release_notes.py, setup.py, config/)
2. Create a commands/ directory with __init__.py that imports all commands
3. Each module should be focused on a single command domain
4. Reduce the main commands.py to just importing and registering commands

This will improve navigation, testability, and make the codebase easier to understand.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:15.156265839+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:15.156265839+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-n6w","title":"Consolidate package release info fetching","description":"The release_notes.py module has get_npm_release_info() and get_pypi_release_info() that follow similar patterns:

1. Both fetch version info from package registries
2. Both use subprocess.run with curl
3. Both parse JSON responses
4. Both format output similarly

Consider:
1. Extract common fetch/parse logic into a generic function
2. Create a registry-based approach for package managers
3. Make it easier to add support for new package managers
4. Improve testability by mocking the generic interface","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:26.254981742+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:26.254981742+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-xmd","title":"Remove or formalize _dict_to_config() in __init__.py","description":"The _dict_to_config() function in __init__.py is documented as 'Temporary bridge function - delegates to validate_config from config.py. Kept for backward compatibility during migration.'

This function is only used once in load_config() and adds an unnecessary layer of indirection. Either:
1. Remove it entirely and call validate_config() directly
2. If backward compatibility is needed, add a proper deprecation notice
3. Add a TODO comment with a timeline for removal

Having temporary code in production for too long is a code smell.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:26.235907687+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:26.235907687+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-cg9","title":"Refactor data_loader.py validation functions","description":"The data_loader.py file has repetitive validation functions (_validate_harness_data, _validate_dependency_data, _validate_preset_data, _validate_method_data) with similar patterns.

Consider:
1. Create a generic validator helper that takes a schema definition
2. Use dataclasses with pydantic-like validation
3. Extract common validation patterns into reusable functions
4. Reduce code duplication from ~100 lines to a single schema-driven approach

The current approach duplicates the same validation logic with slight variations for each data type.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:26.237726308+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:26.237726308+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-70a","title":"Add type hints for module-level caches in data_loader.py","description":"The data_loader.py module uses global module-level caches without proper type hints:



While type hints are present, these global mutable caches are a code smell:
1. Hard to test (can't easily mock or clear in tests)
2. Potential for state pollution between tests
3. Thread-safety concerns if code becomes concurrent

Consider:
1. Move caches to a CacheManager class
2. Make clear_cache() more robust
3. Consider using functools.lru_cache for automatic cache management
4. Add documentation about cache invalidation strategy","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:39.731056023+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:39.731056023+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-p77","title":"Improve execution.py error handling","description":"The run_command_async() function in execution.py has potential issues in error handling:

1. The finally block tries to close _transport but it may not exist
2. If create_subprocess_shell() fails before process is assigned, we get AttributeError
3. No logging of the actual exception type in the except block
4. Timeout handling kills the process but the error message is generic

Consider:
1. Use a more robust pattern for process cleanup
2. Add proper exception logging with traceback
3. Consider using contextlib.asynccontextmanager for better resource management
4. Add unit tests for timeout and error scenarios","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:39.637945749+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:39.637945749+01:00"}

{"id":"reincheck-6kr","title":"Reduce cyclomatic complexity in setup command","description":"The setup() command in commands.py is extremely complex (300+ lines) with multiple nested conditions and concerns mixed together:

1. Validation logic (_validate_setup_options)
2. Interactive preset selection (_select_preset_interactive_with_fallback)
3. Harness selection (_select_harnesses_interactive_with_fallback)
4. Method resolution (_resolve_all_methods)
5. Config writing (_write_agent_config)
6. Installation planning (_get_harnesses_to_install, plan_install)
7. Installation execution (_execute_installation_with_progress)

Consider:
1. Extract each major phase into its own function
2. Create a SetupWorkflow class to orchestrate the process
3. Reduce nesting levels
4. Improve testability by isolating concerns","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"852741+Megatherium@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:39.628269852+01:00","created_by":"Megatherium","updated_at":"2026-02-09T23:21:39.628269852+01:00"}

2. adapter.py - get_effective_method() has parameter overrides: dict[str, str] | None but could use TypedDict

Adding better type hints would:

3. Improve documentation

{"id":"reincheck-r80","title":"Improve error message consistency","description":"Error messages across the codebase are inconsistent in style and format:

1. Some use click.echo() with err=True
2. Some use _logging.error()
3. Some use ConfigError with detailed messages
4. Some use generic 'Error: ' prefix
5. Some have context suggestions, others don't

Examples of inconsistency:
- config.py: 'Config must be a JSON object, got list'
- commands.py: 'Error: ' prefix
- installer.py: Various ConfigError formats
- data_loader.py: 'Invalid harnesses data file: missing top-level'

Consider:
1. Create a consistent error formatting helper
2. Define a style guide for error messages

{"id":"reincheck-xp4","title":"Refinement: Fix parameter count and error handling in setup.py","description":"
## Defects Found in refactored setup.py (reincheck-6kr)

### 1. Parameter Count Smell (HIGH PRIORITY)
Multiple helper functions have excessive parameters (>7), violating clean code principles:

- `_display_dry_run()` (line 713) - 10 parameters
- `_execute_installation_flow()` (line 823) - 9 parameters  
- `_resolve_install_methods()` (line 588) - 8 parameters

**Required fix**: Create a `SetupContext` dataclass to hold shared state (presets, methods, harnesses, overrides, debug, yes, verbose) and pass it instead of individual parameters.

### 2. Inconsistent Error Handling (HIGH PRIORITY)
Mix of exception raising vs `sys.exit()` calls makes the code hard to test and maintain:

- `_resolve_selected_preset()` calls `sys.exit()` at lines 943-944, 961
- `_execute_installation_flow()` calls `sys.exit()` at lines 862, 877, 883, 893
- `_write_config_with_backup()` calls `sys.exit()` at lines 805, 820

**Required fix**: 
- Raise `ConfigError` consistently in helper functions
- Handle all `sys.exit()` calls in the main `setup()` function
- Maintain existing exit codes for backward compatibility

### 3. Code Duplication (MEDIUM PRIORITY)
Lines 942-944 duplicate validation already present in `_validate_setup_options()`:

```python
if preset is None and not sys.stdin.isatty():
    click.echo("Error: --preset is required.", err=True)
    sys.exit(EXIT_CONFIG_ERROR)
```

This logic exists at lines 74-76 in `_validate_setup_options()`.

**Required fix**: Remove duplicate check from main `setup()` function (lines 942-944).

### 4. Presentation Logic in Business Functions (LOW PRIORITY)
`_execute_installation_flow()` directly calls `click.echo()` for error messages (lines 861, 890, 895), making it hard to test in isolation.

**Required fix**: Consider returning status/error tuples and let caller handle display, OR keep as-is if test coverage is sufficient.

## Files Modified
- reincheck/commands/setup.py

## Success Criteria
1. All helper functions have ≤7 parameters
2. Error handling uses exceptions only, no `sys.exit()` in helpers
3. No duplicate validation logic
4. All 304 tests still pass
5. No functional behavior changes (exit codes, output messages)
